questions.title: Prenatal Assessment
questions.text: A pregnant woman at 20 weeks gestation reports feeling fetal movement. This is an example of which presumptive sign of pregnancy?
questions.rationale: Quickening refers to the maternal perception of fetal movement for the first time, typically occurring between 16-20 weeks in a first pregnancy and possibly earlier in subsequent pregnancies. Chadwick's sign is the bluish-purple discoloration of the vagina and cervix due to increased vascularity, occurring around 8-12 weeks. Ballottement is a passive fetal movement elicited by the examiner pushing on the uterus, not a sensation felt by the mother. Braxton Hicks contractions are intermittent, irregular uterine contractions that may begin as early as the second trimester but are not related to the sensation of fetal movement.

questions.title: Prenatal Nutrition
questions.text: A pregnant client at 12 weeks gestation asks the nurse about foods that are good sources of folic acid. Which food should the nurse recommend to this client?
questions.rationale: Green leafy vegetables such as spinach, kale, and collard greens are excellent sources of folate (natural folic acid). Adequate folic acid intake before and during early pregnancy helps prevent neural tube defects, which form during the first 28 days after conception. Dairy products are good sources of calcium but not particularly high in folate. Lean meats provide protein and iron but are not significant sources of folate. While citrus fruits do contain some folate, green leafy vegetables generally have higher concentrations.

questions.title: Preeclampsia Management
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a pregnant woman at 36 weeks gestation who has been diagnosed with preeclampsia. Which finding would indicate worsening of her condition?

choices.text: New onset of headache and visual disturbances

questions.rationale: New onset of headache and visual disturbances in a woman with preeclampsia indicates cerebral involvement and suggests progression to severe preeclampsia, which requires immediate medical attention to prevent complications such as eclampsia (seizures). A blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg meets the minimum diagnostic criteria for preeclampsia but does not necessarily indicate worsening of the condition if it was previously higher. Ankle edema is common in normal pregnancy and is not a diagnostic criterion for preeclampsia. Proteinuria of 300 mg/24 hours is the minimum threshold for diagnosing preeclampsia and does not necessarily indicate worsening if it was previously higher.

questions.title: Labor Progress
questions.text: A primiparous client is in active labor. Her cervix is 7 cm dilated, 100% effaced, and the fetal station is +1. Based on this assessment, in which stage and phase of labor is this client?
questions.rationale: The client is in the first stage, active phase of labor. The first stage of labor begins with the onset of regular contractions and ends with complete cervical dilation (10 cm). The active phase of the first stage is characterized by more rapid cervical dilation, typically from 4-7 cm to 10 cm. The client's cervix is 7 cm dilated, which places her in the active phase of the first stage. The second stage begins with complete cervical dilation (10 cm) and ends with the birth of the baby, which has not occurred yet based on the information provided.

questions.title: Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring
questions.text: A nurse is monitoring the fetal heart rate (FHR) of a laboring client. The nurse notes a FHR of 100 beats per minute that drops to 80 beats per minute during a contraction and returns to 100 beats per minute after the contraction ends. How should the nurse interpret this finding?
questions.rationale: Early decelerations are characterized by a gradual decrease in fetal heart rate that begins at the onset of a contraction, reaches its lowest point at the peak of the contraction, and returns to baseline by the end of the contraction. They are caused by fetal head compression during contractions and are considered a normal physiological response, not a sign of fetal distress. Late decelerations begin after the onset of a contraction, reach their lowest point after the peak of the contraction, and return to baseline after the contraction ends; they are associated with uteroplacental insufficiency. Variable decelerations have an abrupt onset and offset, varying in timing and appearance, and are associated with umbilical cord compression. Prolonged decelerations last longer than 2 minutes but less than 10 minutes.

questions.title: Postpartum Assessment
questions.text: A nurse is assessing a client who is 2 hours postpartum. Which finding would be considered normal?

choices.text: Firm fundus located at the level of the umbilicus

questions.rationale: A firm fundus located at the level of the umbilicus is a normal finding in the immediate postpartum period. The firm consistency indicates that the uterus is contracted, which helps control bleeding from the placental site. Lochia rubra (bright red vaginal discharge) is normal, but the presence of large clots is abnormal and may indicate excessive bleeding. A temperature of 101.2°F (38.4°C) at 2 hours postpartum is elevated and may indicate infection or dehydration; normal temperature range is 97.7-99.5°F (36.5-37.5°C). A soft, boggy uterus indicates uterine atony, which can lead to postpartum hemorrhage and requires immediate intervention.

questions.title: Newborn Assessment
questions.text: A nurse is performing an assessment on a newborn who is 2 hours old. Which finding would require immediate intervention?

choices.text: Respiratory rate of 40 breaths per minute
Acrocyanosis of the hands and feet
Heart rate of 100 beats per minute

questions.rationale: Absence of bowel sounds in a 2-hour-old newborn requires immediate intervention as it may indicate a gastrointestinal obstruction or abnormality. Bowel sounds should be present within 15-30 minutes after birth. A respiratory rate of 40 breaths per minute is within the normal range for a newborn (30-60 breaths per minute). Acrocyanosis, or bluish discoloration of the hands and feet, is a normal finding in the first 24-48 hours of life due to peripheral vasoconstriction and immature circulation. A heart rate of 100 beats per minute is at the lower end of the normal range for a newborn (100-160 beats per minute) but is not immediately concerning if the infant is otherwise stable.

questions.title: Postpartum Complications
questions.text: A postpartum client develops a temperature of 102.5°F (39.2°C) on day 3 after a vaginal delivery. She reports lower abdominal pain and has a foul-smelling lochia. The nurse suspects which condition?
questions.rationale: Endometritis, an infection of the endometrium (lining of the uterus), is characterized by fever, lower abdominal pain, and foul-smelling lochia. It typically presents on days 3-5 postpartum. Mastitis, an infection of the breast tissue, presents with unilateral breast pain, redness, warmth, and swelling, usually occurring later (7-10 days postpartum). A urinary tract infection would typically present with dysuria, frequency, urgency, and possibly fever, but not necessarily with foul-smelling lochia. Deep vein thrombosis would present with unilateral leg pain, swelling, warmth, and possibly fever, but not with foul-smelling lochia.

questions.title: Gestational Diabetes
questions.text: A pregnant client at 30 weeks gestation has been diagnosed with gestational diabetes. Which complication is the client at increased risk of developing?
questions.rationale: Fetal macrosomia (excessive fetal growth resulting in a birth weight >4000g or >8.8 pounds) is a common complication of gestational diabetes. The elevated maternal glucose levels cross the placenta, causing the fetus to produce more insulin, which acts as a growth factor. While gestational diabetes is associated with some increase in preterm labor risk, it is not as direct or common a complication as macrosomia. Placenta previa (abnormal placental implantation over the cervical os) is not directly associated with gestational diabetes. Intrauterine growth restriction (poor fetal growth) is actually contrary to what is typically seen with gestational diabetes, which more commonly causes excessive fetal growth.

questions.title: Medication in Pregnancy
questions.text: A pregnant woman at 36 weeks gestation is admitted for preterm labor. The healthcare provider orders betamethasone. The nurse understands that this medication is administered for which purpose?
questions.rationale: Betamethasone is a corticosteroid administered to accelerate fetal lung maturity by stimulating the production of pulmonary surfactant. It is typically given between 24 and 34 weeks gestation when preterm delivery is anticipated, to reduce the risk of respiratory distress syndrome in the newborn. Although this scenario mentions 36 weeks, which is technically late preterm, the medication might still be considered. Medications used to stop uterine contractions (tocolytics) include nifedipine, indomethacin, or terbutaline, not betamethasone. Antibiotics would be used to treat maternal infection, not betamethasone. Medications used to induce labor include oxytocin or prostaglandins, not betamethasone.

questions.title: Antepartum Bleeding
questions.text: A pregnant client at 34 weeks gestation presents with sudden, painless vaginal bleeding. On assessment, the nurse notes a soft uterus with normal tone and a fetal heart rate of 140 beats per minute. Which complication does the nurse suspect?
questions.rationale: Placenta previa is characterized by sudden, painless vaginal bleeding, typically bright red in color. The uterus remains soft with normal tone because the bleeding is from the placental site, not from uterine contractions or disruption of the placenta. Placental abruption typically presents with painful vaginal bleeding, a board-like rigid uterus, and often fetal distress. Uterine rupture typically presents with severe abdominal pain, cessation of contractions, recession of the presenting part, and signs of maternal shock; it is more common in women with a history of cesarean delivery or other uterine surgery. Preterm labor presents with regular uterine contractions that cause cervical change before 37 weeks gestation; vaginal bleeding is not a primary symptom unless accompanied by another complication.

questions.title: Neonatal Jaundice
questions.text: A newborn develops jaundice at 48 hours of life with a total serum bilirubin level of 12 mg/dL. The nurse anticipates which treatment?
questions.rationale: Phototherapy is the most appropriate treatment for a term newborn with physiologic jaundice and a total serum bilirubin level of 12 mg/dL at 48 hours of life. Phototherapy uses light of specific wavelengths to convert unconjugated bilirubin into water-soluble forms that can be excreted. Exchange transfusion is reserved for severe hyperbilirubinemia not responsive to phototherapy or when bilirubin levels are dangerously high (typically >20 mg/dL in a term newborn), risking kernicterus. Phenobarbital can increase the rate of bilirubin conjugation in the liver but is rarely used for neonatal jaundice due to its side effects and the availability of more effective treatments. Discontinuation of breastfeeding is not recommended as a routine treatment for neonatal jaundice. While 'breastfeeding jaundice' can occur due to insufficient milk intake in the first few days, the appropriate intervention is to support more frequent breastfeeding, not discontinuation.

questions.title: Breastfeeding Education
questions.text: A postpartum client who plans to breastfeed asks the nurse how she will know if her baby is getting enough milk. Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.text: "Your baby should nurse for at least 30 minutes on each breast."
"Your breasts should feel completely empty after each feeding."
"Your baby should have at least 6-8 wet diapers and 3-4 stools per day after day 4."
"You should supplement with formula after each feeding to ensure adequate nutrition."

questions.rationale: An adequate number of wet diapers (6-8 per day) and stools (3-4 per day) after day 4 is one of the most reliable indicators that a breastfed baby is receiving enough milk. The duration of nursing varies greatly among infants and is not a reliable indicator of adequate intake; some efficient babies may complete a feeding in 5-10 minutes. Breasts are never completely empty after a feeding, as milk production is continuous. Routine supplementation with formula after breastfeeding is not recommended for healthy, term infants and may interfere with establishing breastfeeding.

questions.title: Rh Incompatibility
questions.text: An Rh-negative pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation with no anti-Rh antibodies is scheduled to receive Rh immune globulin (RhoGAM). The client asks why this is necessary. Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?

choices.text: "It will protect your baby from developing jaundice after birth."
"It will prevent your body from producing antibodies that could harm future pregnancies if this baby is Rh-positive."
"It will treat any Rh incompatibility that has already developed in this pregnancy."
"It will increase your iron levels to prevent anemia in pregnancy."

questions.rationale: Rh immune globulin (RhoGAM) is administered to Rh-negative women during pregnancy (typically at 28 weeks) and after delivery of an Rh-positive baby to prevent sensitization, which is the development of maternal antibodies against the Rh factor. If sensitization occurs, in future pregnancies with an Rh-positive fetus, these maternal antibodies can cross the placenta and attack fetal red blood cells, leading to hemolytic disease of the newborn. RhoGAM does not directly prevent neonatal jaundice in the current pregnancy, although preventing hemolytic disease in future pregnancies would prevent the associated jaundice. RhoGAM is preventive, not therapeutic; it cannot treat Rh incompatibility that has already developed. RhoGAM is an immune globulin, not an iron supplement, and does not affect maternal iron levels or anemia.

questions.title: Postpartum Depression
questions.text: A postpartum client at her 6-week check-up reports feeling sad, overwhelmed, and unable to care for her baby. She says she thinks about harming herself and the baby. Which nursing action has highest priority?

choices.text: Reassure her that these feelings are normal and will pass
Suggest that she get more rest and have family members help with the baby
Refer her to a support group for new mothers
Immediately report her statements to the healthcare provider and ensure she is not left alone

questions.rationale: The highest priority action is to immediately report the client's statements about self-harm and harming the baby to the healthcare provider and ensure she is not left alone. These statements indicate potential postpartum depression with psychosis or suicidal/homicidal ideation, which is a psychiatric emergency requiring immediate intervention to ensure the safety of both mother and baby. It would be inappropriate and potentially dangerous to reassure her that these feelings are normal and will pass; thoughts of harming oneself or one's baby are not normal postpartum feelings and require immediate psychiatric evaluation. While rest and family support may be helpful adjuncts to treatment, they are insufficient interventions for a client expressing thoughts of harm. A support group for new mothers would be inadequate for someone expressing thoughts of self-harm or harming her baby; this client needs immediate professional psychiatric evaluation and possibly hospitalization.

questions.title: Hyperemesis Gravidarum
questions.text: A pregnant client at 10 weeks gestation is hospitalized with hyperemesis gravidarum. Which assessment finding would the nurse expect to see in this client?
questions.rationale: Hypokalemia (low serum potassium) is an expected finding in a client with hyperemesis gravidarum due to excessive vomiting, which leads to loss of electrolytes, including potassium. Other electrolyte imbalances, such as hyponatremia, may also be present. Weight gain would not be expected; instead, weight loss is typical due to inability to retain nutrition. Decreased urine specific gravity would indicate dilute urine, which is not consistent with the dehydration that occurs in hyperemesis gravidarum; increased urine specific gravity would be expected. Normal skin turgor would not be expected; poor skin turgor (tenting) due to dehydration would be more likely.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD) reports increasing fatigue and shortness of breath.  Which laboratory result should the nurse prioritize reviewing?
questions.rationale: Fatigue and shortness of breath are common symptoms of anemia, a frequent complication of CKD.  Checking the hemoglobin level (B) is crucial to assess for anemia. While the other options are relevant to CKD management, anemia is the most likely explanation for these symptoms.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient is experiencing heavy vaginal bleeding. Which nursing interventions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage requires prompt interventions to control bleeding.  Fundal massage (A) helps to contract the uterus, reducing blood flow. Frequent vital signs (B) are essential to monitor for shock. Oxytocin (C) is commonly used to stimulate uterine contractions. A distended bladder (E) can impede uterine contraction, so assessment is vital.  While monitoring urine output (F) is important for overall hydration, it isn’t a direct intervention for immediate postpartum hemorrhage management. Encouraging ambulation (D) is appropriate in many cases, but the priority is controlling the bleeding first.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding. Which nursing actions are appropriate?

choices.text: Assess the fundus for firmness and position.
Measure the amount of blood loss.
Encourage the patient to ambulate frequently.
Apply ice packs to the perineum.

questions.rationale: Heavy postpartum bleeding requires prompt assessment and intervention. Assessing fundal firmness and position helps identify uterine atony (A), a common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. Measuring blood loss accurately is crucial (B). Oxytocin is often used to stimulate uterine contractions (C). Monitoring vital signs helps detect signs of hypovolemic shock (D). E and F are less immediate interventions in the context of heavy bleeding.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Ventilator alarms high pressure.  The patient is receiving mechanical ventilation and suddenly exhibits increased respiratory rate and labored breathing.  What is the priority action?

choices.text:
Immediately suction the patient's airway.
A) Check the ventilator tubing for kinks or obstructions.
C) Assess the patient's oxygen saturation.
D) Increase the ventilator's tidal volume.
Correct Answer: B
Rationale: High pressure ventilator alarms typically indicate an obstruction in the airway or ventilator tubing.  Checking for kinks or obstructions is the immediate priority to alleviate the high pressure and improve ventilation.  While suctioning (A) might be necessary, it's not the first step; the cause of the obstruction must be identified and resolved first.  (C) is important but doesn't address the root cause, and (D) could worsen the situation if the problem is an obstruction.

--- End Question ---
Question Number: 3.
Category: Neurology
Type: Select All That Apply
Difficulty: Medium
Question: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L. Which assessment findings would the nurse expect to find in a patient with hyperkalemia?
A)  Muscle weakness

questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (high potassium) causes alterations in cardiac rhythm (bradycardia, peaked T waves), neuromuscular excitability (hyperreflexia), and potentially muscle weakness (although profound weakness can also indicate hypokalemia). Hypotension is not a direct effect of hyperkalemia, and bowel sounds are not directly related to potassium levels.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding and dizziness.  Which actions should the nurse take?

choices.text: Check the patient's hemoglobin and hematocrit levels.

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage is a serious complication.  Assessing vital signs (A) is crucial to monitor the patient's stability. Fundal massage (B) helps to contract the uterus and control bleeding. Oxygen (C) is administered to increase oxygenation to tissues.  Notifying the physician (E) is essential due to the severity of the situation. Checking hemoglobin and hematocrit (F) helps to assess the extent of blood loss. Increasing IV fluids (D) might be considered but is not the immediate priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding and is saturating a perineal pad in less than 15 minutes.  Her blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg. What is the priority nursing action?
questions.rationale: The patient is exhibiting signs of postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) with hypotension. Rapid fluid resuscitation is crucial to stabilize the patient's circulatory status. While the other options are appropriate interventions for PPH management, restoring blood volume is the immediate priority to prevent shock.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy bleeding. Which actions should the nurse take?
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage is a serious complication.  Assessing fundal height and firmness helps identify the cause of bleeding.  Monitoring vital signs is crucial to detect signs of shock. Fundal massage helps to contract the uterus, reducing bleeding. Oxytocin is a medication used to stimulate uterine contractions.  Preparing for a blood transfusion might be necessary if bleeding is severe, but assessment and intervention should occur first. Increasing IV fluids is not typically the first action taken.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding.  Her fundus is boggy and midline.  Which nursing action should be taken FIRST?
questions.rationale: A boggy, midline fundus indicates uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage.  The FIRST action is to massage the fundus (B) to stimulate uterine contractions and reduce bleeding. Assessing vital signs (A) is important but should follow the immediate action of massaging the fundus to control bleeding. While notifying the physician (C) and administering oxytocin (D) are necessary, they are secondary to the immediate intervention of fundal massage.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient is experiencing excessive vaginal bleeding.  Which nursing actions are appropriate?

choices.text: Monitor for signs of hypovolemic shock.
Place the patient in Trendelenburg position.

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage requires immediate action.  Fundal massage helps to contract the uterus and decrease bleeding.  Vital signs monitoring is crucial to detect early signs of hypovolemic shock. Oxytocin is a medication used to stimulate uterine contractions.  Monitoring for shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor) is critical.  Trendelenburg position is generally avoided as it can impede venous return and worsen bleeding.  Intravenous fluids may be necessary later to replace fluid loss but are not the immediate priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with hypothyroidism is experiencing fatigue and weight gain. Which laboratory value would the nurse expect to be abnormal?
questions.rationale: In hypothyroidism, the thyroid gland doesn't produce enough thyroid hormones (T3 and T4), leading to an increase in TSH.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Upon admission, a patient reports taking warfarin (Coumadin) daily. Which laboratory test is essential to monitor?
questions.rationale: INR is the most commonly used test to monitor warfarin's anticoagulant effect.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy bleeding and saturates a perineal pad in 15 minutes. What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) is a life-threatening emergency.  Immediate notification of the physician (C) is the priority to initiate rapid intervention.  While the other options (A, B, D) are important aspects of managing PPH, they must follow prompt physician notification.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which signs indicate potential complications during labor?

choices.text: Decreased variability in fetal heart rate.
Late decelerations in fetal heart rate.

questions.rationale: Fetal bradycardia (A), maternal fever (B), decreased variability in fetal heart rate (C), late decelerations (D), and increased maternal blood pressure (E) are all signs of potential complications during labor, requiring immediate intervention.  Frequent, strong contractions (F) may be normal, depending on the context.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding and a sudden drop in blood pressure.  What is the nurse's immediate priority?

choices.text: Assess the fundus for firmness and position.

questions.rationale: This suggests postpartum hemorrhage, a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate medical intervention.  While the other actions are appropriate parts of management, notifying the physician immediately is the priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Ventilator alarms high pressure.  The patient is receiving mechanical ventilation and suddenly exhibits increased respiratory rate and labored breathing. Which actions should the nurse immediately take?  Select all that apply.

choices.text: Check for kinks in the ventilator tubing.

questions.rationale: A high-pressure alarm on a ventilator indicates increased resistance to airflow.  This could be due to several factors, including kinks in the tubing (A), secretions in the airway requiring suctioning (B), patient coughing or struggling against the ventilator (requiring assessment of oxygen saturation (C) and ventilator settings (D) to rule out mechanical issues or patient-ventilator asynchrony).  Administering a bronchodilator (E) might be considered after assessing the situation, but is not an immediate action.  Increasing tidal volume (F) without understanding the cause of the high pressure could worsen the situation, potentially causing lung injury (volutrauma).

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD) reports increasing fatigue and shortness of breath.  Which laboratory result should the nurse prioritize reviewing?
questions.rationale: Fatigue and shortness of breath are common symptoms of anemia, a frequent complication of CKD.  Checking the hemoglobin level (B) is crucial to assess for anemia. While the other options are relevant to CKD management, anemia is the most likely explanation for these symptoms.

A distended bladder (E) can impede uterine contraction, so assessment is vital.  While monitoring urine output (F) is important for overall hydration, it isn’t a direct intervention for immediate postpartum hemorrhage management. Encouraging ambulation (D) is appropriate in many cases, but the priority is controlling the bleeding first.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: During labor, a patient's membranes rupture.  What is the nurse's priority assessment?

choices.text: Amount and color of amniotic fluid.

questions.rationale: Following rupture of membranes, the priority is to assess fetal heart rate (A) to detect any signs of fetal distress such as bradycardia or variable decelerations which may indicate umbilical cord compression. While the other assessments are also important, the fetal well-being is paramount.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is taking warfarin.  What is the most important laboratory value to monitor?
questions.rationale: Warfarin is an anticoagulant.  The INR (A) is the standardized measure of the PT used to monitor warfarin therapy.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding.  Her fundus is boggy and above the umbilicus. Which intervention should the nurse perform first?
questions.rationale: A boggy, elevated fundus indicates uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage.  Fundal massage is the immediate intervention to stimulate uterine contractions and reduce bleeding. While assessing vital signs (C), administering oxytocin (B), and notifying the physician (D) are also necessary, massaging the fundus is the first action to directly address the bleeding.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with hypothyroidism reports persistent fatigue and cold intolerance. Which laboratory finding would support this diagnosis?
questions.rationale: In hypothyroidism, the pituitary gland produces more TSH in an attempt to stimulate the thyroid to produce more hormones. This leads to elevated TSH levels.  Options B, C, and D are associated with hyperthyroidism or other endocrine disorders.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Following a postpartum hemorrhage, which interventions are appropriate?

choices.text: Monitor for signs of hypovolemic shock

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage requires immediate interventions to control bleeding. Fundal massage (A) helps contract the uterus. Oxytocin (B) is a uterotonic drug that stimulates uterine contractions.  Monitoring vital signs (C) is essential to detect hypovolemic shock. A distended bladder (D) can impede uterine contraction, worsening bleeding. Close monitoring for signs of hypovolemic shock (F) is crucial.  While IV fluids (E) may be necessary for fluid resuscitation, this is less immediate than addressing uterine atony and monitoring for shock.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Following a forceps delivery, the neonate has a cephalohematoma.

choices.text: Apply ice packs to the affected area.

questions.rationale: A cephalohematoma is a collection of blood between the skull and periosteum. It typically resolves spontaneously.  The primary concern is the risk of jaundice (B) due to the potential for hemolysis. While ice packs (A) might provide comfort, they are not essential. Vitamin K (C) is given prophylactically to prevent hemorrhagic disease of the newborn, unrelated to the cephalohematoma. A cranial ultrasound (D) may be indicated if there are other concerns, but jaundice is the primary risk.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy bleeding and is saturating a perineal pad in less than 15 minutes.  Which interventions should the nurse perform?

choices.text: Assess the fundus for firmness and position.

questions.rationale: Heavy postpartum bleeding (postpartum hemorrhage) is a life-threatening emergency. Assessing fundal firmness and position (A) helps identify the cause of bleeding. Fundal massage (B) helps to contract the uterus and reduce bleeding. Monitoring vital signs (D) is essential to assess for shock.  Notifying the physician (E) is crucial, and administering oxytocin (F) as prescribed helps to contract the uterus. Increasing IV fluids (C) is important to combat hypovolemic shock but is secondary to the other actions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding.  The nurse's priority action is to:
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage is a serious complication. Fundal massage (A) is the immediate priority to help control bleeding by contracting the uterine muscles.  While monitoring vital signs (B), administering oxytocin (C), and assessing the perineum (D) are all important, fundal massage is the first intervention to address the immediate threat.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is prescribed warfarin.  Which laboratory value should the nurse monitor most closely?
questions.rationale: Warfarin is an anticoagulant, and the INR (C) is used to monitor its effectiveness and prevent excessive bleeding.  While PT (A) and aPTT (B) are related to coagulation, INR provides a standardized measure for warfarin therapy.  CBC (D) is not directly related to warfarin's therapeutic effect.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newborn infant exhibits signs of respiratory distress.  What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Assessing oxygen saturation (B) is the priority to determine the severity of respiratory compromise. While all the options are important, determining the level of hypoxemia guides immediate interventions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient in labor is receiving an epidural. Which assessment findings require immediate attention?
questions.rationale: Hypotension (A), fetal bradycardia (B), and respiratory depression (D) are serious complications that may occur with epidural analgesia and necessitate immediate action.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Upon admission, a patient reports taking warfarin (Coumadin) daily. Which laboratory test is essential to monitor?
questions.rationale: INR is the most commonly used test to monitor warfarin's anticoagulant effect.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with chronic kidney disease (CKD) reports increasing fatigue and shortness of breath.  Which laboratory result should the nurse prioritize reviewing?
questions.rationale: Fatigue and shortness of breath are common symptoms of anemia, a frequent complication of CKD.  Checking the hemoglobin level (B) is crucial to assess for anemia. While the other options are relevant to CKD management, anemia is the most likely explanation for these symptoms.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient is experiencing heavy vaginal bleeding. Which nursing interventions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage requires prompt interventions to control bleeding.  Fundal massage (A) helps to contract the uterus, reducing blood flow. Frequent vital signs (B) are essential to monitor for shock. Oxytocin (C) is commonly used to stimulate uterine contractions. A distended bladder (E) can impede uterine contraction, so assessment is vital.  While monitoring urine output (F) is important for overall hydration, it isn’t a direct intervention for immediate postpartum hemorrhage management. Encouraging ambulation (D) is appropriate in many cases, but the priority is controlling the bleeding first.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding. Which nursing actions are appropriate?

choices.text: Assess the fundus for firmness and position.
Measure the amount of blood loss.
Encourage the patient to ambulate frequently.
Apply ice packs to the perineum.

questions.rationale: Heavy postpartum bleeding requires prompt assessment and intervention. Assessing fundal firmness and position helps identify uterine atony (A), a common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. Measuring blood loss accurately is crucial (B). Oxytocin is often used to stimulate uterine contractions (C). Monitoring vital signs helps detect signs of hypovolemic shock (D). E and F are less immediate interventions in the context of heavy bleeding.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Ventilator alarms high pressure.  The patient is receiving mechanical ventilation and suddenly exhibits increased respiratory rate and labored breathing.  What is the priority action?

choices.text:
Immediately suction the patient's airway.
A) Check the ventilator tubing for kinks or obstructions.
C) Assess the patient's oxygen saturation.
D) Increase the ventilator's tidal volume.
Correct Answer: B
Rationale: High pressure ventilator alarms typically indicate an obstruction in the airway or ventilator tubing.  Checking for kinks or obstructions is the immediate priority to alleviate the high pressure and improve ventilation.  While suctioning (A) might be necessary, it's not the first step; the cause of the obstruction must be identified and resolved first.  (C) is important but doesn't address the root cause, and (D) could worsen the situation if the problem is an obstruction.

--- End Question ---
Question Number: 3.
Category: Neurology
Type: Select All That Apply
Difficulty: Medium
Question: Potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L. Which assessment findings would the nurse expect to find in a patient with hyperkalemia?
A)  Muscle weakness

questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia (high potassium) causes alterations in cardiac rhythm (bradycardia, peaked T waves), neuromuscular excitability (hyperreflexia), and potentially muscle weakness (although profound weakness can also indicate hypokalemia). Hypotension is not a direct effect of hyperkalemia, and bowel sounds are not directly related to potassium levels.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding and dizziness.  Which actions should the nurse take?

choices.text: Check the patient's hemoglobin and hematocrit levels.

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage is a serious complication.  Assessing vital signs (A) is crucial to monitor the patient's stability. Fundal massage (B) helps to contract the uterus and control bleeding. Oxygen (C) is administered to increase oxygenation to tissues.  Notifying the physician (E) is essential due to the severity of the situation. Checking hemoglobin and hematocrit (F) helps to assess the extent of blood loss. Increasing IV fluids (D) might be considered but is not the immediate priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding and is saturating a perineal pad in less than 15 minutes.  Her blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg. What is the priority nursing action?
questions.rationale: The patient is exhibiting signs of postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) with hypotension. Rapid fluid resuscitation is crucial to stabilize the patient's circulatory status. While the other options are appropriate interventions for PPH management, restoring blood volume is the immediate priority to prevent shock.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy bleeding. Which actions should the nurse take?
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage is a serious complication.  Assessing fundal height and firmness helps identify the cause of bleeding.  Monitoring vital signs is crucial to detect signs of shock. Fundal massage helps to contract the uterus, reducing bleeding. Oxytocin is a medication used to stimulate uterine contractions.  Preparing for a blood transfusion might be necessary if bleeding is severe, but assessment and intervention should occur first. Increasing IV fluids is not typically the first action taken.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding.  Her fundus is boggy and midline.  Which nursing action should be taken FIRST?
questions.rationale: A boggy, midline fundus indicates uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage.  The FIRST action is to massage the fundus (B) to stimulate uterine contractions and reduce bleeding. Assessing vital signs (A) is important but should follow the immediate action of massaging the fundus to control bleeding. While notifying the physician (C) and administering oxytocin (D) are necessary, they are secondary to the immediate intervention of fundal massage.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient is experiencing excessive vaginal bleeding.  Which nursing actions are appropriate?

choices.text: Monitor for signs of hypovolemic shock.
Place the patient in Trendelenburg position.

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage requires immediate action.  Fundal massage helps to contract the uterus and decrease bleeding.  Vital signs monitoring is crucial to detect early signs of hypovolemic shock. Oxytocin is a medication used to stimulate uterine contractions.  Monitoring for shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor) is critical.  Trendelenburg position is generally avoided as it can impede venous return and worsen bleeding.  Intravenous fluids may be necessary later to replace fluid loss but are not the immediate priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient with hypothyroidism is experiencing fatigue and weight gain. Which laboratory value would the nurse expect to be abnormal?
questions.rationale: In hypothyroidism, the thyroid gland doesn't produce enough thyroid hormones (T3 and T4), leading to an increase in TSH.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy bleeding and saturates a perineal pad in 15 minutes. What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) is a life-threatening emergency.  Immediate notification of the physician (C) is the priority to initiate rapid intervention.  While the other options (A, B, D) are important aspects of managing PPH, they must follow prompt physician notification.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Which signs indicate potential complications during labor?

choices.text: Decreased variability in fetal heart rate.
Late decelerations in fetal heart rate.

questions.rationale: Fetal bradycardia (A), maternal fever (B), decreased variability in fetal heart rate (C), late decelerations (D), and increased maternal blood pressure (E) are all signs of potential complications during labor, requiring immediate intervention.  Frequent, strong contractions (F) may be normal, depending on the context.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is taking warfarin.  What is the most important laboratory value to monitor?
questions.rationale: Warfarin is an anticoagulant.  The INR (A) is the standardized measure of the PT used to monitor warfarin therapy.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A postpartum patient reports heavy vaginal bleeding and a sudden drop in blood pressure.  What is the nurse's immediate priority?

choices.text: Assess the fundus for firmness and position.

questions.rationale: This suggests postpartum hemorrhage, a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate medical intervention.  While the other actions are appropriate parts of management, notifying the physician immediately is the priority.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: Ventilator alarms high pressure.  The patient is receiving mechanical ventilation and suddenly exhibits increased respiratory rate and labored breathing. Which actions should the nurse immediately take?  Select all that apply.

choices.text: Check for kinks in the ventilator tubing.

questions.rationale: A high-pressure alarm on a ventilator indicates increased resistance to airflow.  This could be due to several factors, including kinks in the tubing (A), secretions in the airway requiring suctioning (B), patient coughing or struggling against the ventilator (requiring assessment of oxygen saturation (C) and ventilator settings (D) to rule out mechanical issues or patient-ventilator asynchrony).  Administering a bronchodilator (E) might be considered after assessing the situation, but is not an immediate action.  Increasing tidal volume (F) without understanding the cause of the high pressure could worsen the situation, potentially causing lung injury (volutrauma).

While the other assessments are also important, the fetal well-being is paramount.

While monitoring vital signs (B), administering oxytocin (C), and assessing the perineum (D) are all important, fundal massage is the first intervention to address the immediate threat.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient is prescribed warfarin.  Which laboratory value should the nurse monitor most closely?
questions.rationale: Warfarin is an anticoagulant, and the INR (C) is used to monitor its effectiveness and prevent excessive bleeding.  While PT (A) and aPTT (B) are related to coagulation, INR provides a standardized measure for warfarin therapy.  CBC (D) is not directly related to warfarin's therapeutic effect.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A newborn infant exhibits signs of respiratory distress.  What is the nurse's priority action?
questions.rationale: Assessing oxygen saturation (B) is the priority to determine the severity of respiratory compromise. While all the options are important, determining the level of hypoxemia guides immediate interventions.

questions.title: Nursing Question
questions.text: A patient in labor is receiving an epidural. Which assessment findings require immediate attention?
questions.rationale: Hypotension (A), fetal bradycardia (B), and respiratory depression (D) are serious complications that may occur with epidural analgesia and necessitate immediate action.
